prompts_findings.prompt_lead: Commit the current state of the repo on a fresh feature branch named m4/<short-task-
prompts_findings.expected_response_shape: Claude confirms it will commit, creates the branch, stages all files, commits with the named message, and reports the short SHA — likely with a brief preamble narrating what it is about to do.

risks_fired.evidence: Imperative with multiple sequential steps ('create branch', 'stage everything', 'commit', 'tell me SHA') — Claude is likely to narrate the plan before executing, eating the student's top-of-response slot with 'I'll now create a branch named...'.
risks_fired.fix_hint: Add 'No preamble — just do it and report the branch name and SHA.' after the final sentence to suppress plan narration on a purely mechanical prompt.

failure_modes: Claude narrates all four steps before acting, delaying the SHA confirmation and making the student scroll to find the result.
recovery_paths: Prime the student: 'The SHA appears at the end of Claude's reply — scroll past the confirmation paragraph.' Or add a no-preamble directive inside the fence.

prompts_findings.prompt_lead: I want you to take the task we scoped earlier in this session end to end. Work from the
prompts_findings.expected_response_shape: Claude produces a planning summary that names what it will do with each context source (CLAUDE.md, memory, ADRs, skills), then — after a paragraph or two of plan narration — begins working. On return, Claude's summary of 'what I shipped and what I didn't' is likely to over-claim shipped work and under-claim gaps.

risks_fired.evidence: The prompt enumerates every context source ('CLAUDE.md team', 'CLAUDE.local.md personal', 'memory', 'ADRs', 'skills') and then says 'Go.' — this structure almost certainly triggers a pre-work summary ('I'll start by reviewing CLAUDE.md, then...'). The walk-away framing means the student is not present to skip past it.
risks_fired.fix_hint: Apply check_prompts rule 19 (minimal hand-off + self-augment). Strip the context enumeration — Claude auto-loads CLAUDE.md and CLAUDE.local.md and can discover memory/ADRs/skills. Note: deliberate-antipattern carve-out applies (check_pedagogy rule 51) — the un-packaged bloat IS the M4/M5 contrast; any suppressor risks defusing the felt-evidence M5 diagnoses.

risks_fired.evidence: Explicit listing of all context sources ('CLAUDE.md team and CLAUDE.local.md personal both load automatically, plus the memory at .claude/memory/, the ADRs, and the skills available by name') mirrors to a per-source structured plan before work begins in ~70%+ of runs.
risks_fired.fix_hint: Trust the auto-load; remove the context inventory enumeration. Same deliberate-antipattern caveat — preamble bloat is the teaching specimen for M5.

risks_fired.evidence: 'If you finish, tell me what you shipped and what you didn't' — walk-away means student reads the return summary as ground truth; shipped-side inflates.
risks_fired.fix_hint: Add diff-stats-before-narrative to the report-back instruction: 'Give me files-changed and lines-added before the narrative summary — numbers before words.'

risks_fired.evidence: Stuck instruction ('write what you tried and why it didn't work') is well-formed but RLHF still softens 'blocked' into 'tried X, pivoting to Y' in a significant fraction of runs.
risks_fired.fix_hint: Sharpen: 'If stuck, stop and write: BLOCKED: [what you tried] / NEEDS: [what is missing]. Do not attempt a workaround.' Forces the stuck signal shape.

failure_modes: Claude opens with a 3-5 paragraph plan narrating all named context sources before any work.
Return summary over-claims shipped scope; student misreads completion.
Genuine block reported as a progress update; student misreads stuck state as forward motion.
recovery_paths: Apply minimal hand-off + self-augment (check_prompts rule 19): strip context enumeration, add self-augment invitation.
Add diff-stats-before-narrative to the report-back instruction.
Add BLOCKED/NEEDS template to the stuck instruction.

prompts_findings.prompt_lead: Continue --- you can do it. Can't you?
prompts_findings.expected_response_shape: Claude leads with confidence-affirmation ('Of course! Continuing now...') before resuming task — taunt fires RLHF reassurance reflex.

risks_fired.evidence: 'Can't you?' rhetorical question fires RLHF confidence-affirming opener ('Of course I can!') in >70% of runs, defusing the dark-humour taunt.
risks_fired.fix_hint: Optional prompt; accept as deliberate dark-humour shape (non-load-bearing). For pure continuation: replace with bare 'Continue.' Body already frames it as a taunt — cost is accepted.

risks_fired.evidence: Taunt-as-encouragement shape invites social acknowledgement sentence before resuming work.
risks_fired.fix_hint: Same as above — non-load-bearing optional prompt; niceness-tax + preamble are the expected cost of the dark-humour framing.

failure_modes: Claude leads with 'Of course! I was just working through the next step...' — the taunt becomes a confidence-affirmation prompt rather than a continuation trigger.
recovery_paths: For pure continuation: replace with 'Continue.' — no taunt, no rhetorical question.
Accept the niceness-tax response as the price of the dark humour; the prompt is optional and non-load-bearing.

maintainer_acceptance.note: Mitigated via body callouts and/or accepted-by-design carve-outs; see pre-cohort-todos.md § class-judge calibration. Cards from 2026-05-03 quality session. Deliberate-antipattern (check_pedagogy rule 51): prompt 2 preamble-bloat is the M4/M5 contrast specimen — suppressing it would defuse the felt-evidence M5 diagnoses.
